text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 30 May 2021 

Dr Patel  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Patel,  

George Thompson, DOB: 12-03-1941, Hospital No: H847203 NHS No. 9081726345  
Roseview Care Home, 14 Brookside Road, London, W2 5RE 

Medication:  
Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily via crushed tablets in semi-solid; Clobazam 5 mg nocte. Both are being administered by the live-in nurse as per blister pack. No adverse effects reported beyond mild daytime somnolence, which the caregiver feels is unchanged from baseline.

Background and current status:  
George Thompson is an older adult who has been largely bedbound since a hip fracture last year. Most of the clinical history and observations are provided by his daytime nurse and overnight caregiver at the residential unit. There is no history available to suggest a single unifying trigger, and events occur from sleep and wakefulness. He does not drive. Swallow is safe for crushed medications; dietitian input is ongoing for weight maintenance.

Seizure history since the last review:  
The nursing notes and incident forms were reviewed alongside the unit’s electronic log. In Nov he had 5 short seizures in a brief series. In Feb six daytime events were noted, in Mar 5 myoclonic jerks occurred, and in May three generalised tonic-clonic seizures at the centre (staff-witnessed). The caregiver reports that post-event confusion typically lasts 10–20 minutes with gradual return to his baseline alertness. No clear illness, missed doses, or sleep deprivation were identified around these clusters. There has been no head injury, tongue biting has been recorded twice, and there was one urinary incontinence episode in May. 

Assessment and plan:  
- Continue current doses of levetiracetam and clobazam given tolerability and caregiver preference to avoid daytime sedation.  
- The unit has a rescue protocol in place (buccal midazolam 5 mg for clusters or events exceeding 5 minutes); this has not been required this quarter.  
- We have provided the caregivers with a simple event diary template and requested brief smartphone video where feasible to assist characterisation.  
- Routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs) to be arranged through primary care in the next 2–4 weeks, given reduced mobility and polypharmacy.  
- Pressure area care and hydration measures to continue; the nurse will liaise with the falls team regarding safe repositioning after events.

Follow-up:  
We will review him in four months via joint video link with the nurse and caregiver, or sooner if there is any escalation in frequency, prolonged events, or new focal neurological deficits.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: We need to infer current seizure frequency from the letter. The letter provides counts over months since the last review: "In Nov he had 5 short seizures in a brief series. In Feb six daytime events were noted, in Mar 5 myoclonic jerks occurred, and in May three generalised tonic-clonic seizures." These are discrete counts per month across a span of four months with recorded events: Nov (5), Feb (6), Mar (5), May (3). Total seizures mentioned = 5 + 6 + 5 + 3 = 19. The months with events span from November to May inclusive, but the actual months with recorded events are four distinct months. The instruction is to determine current seizure frequency and normalize to allowed formats. Since the letter aggregates counts across several months without exact days and the safest aggregate rate is per month across the inclusive span. The span from Nov to May is 7 months (Nov, Dec, Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May). Therefore, average frequency = 19 over 7 months = 19 per 7 months. This matches allowed format '<value1> per <value2> month'. Select highest frequency rule doesn't change this as we only have monthly totals. Not seizure-free. Provide a verbatim quote supporting the counts.
seizure_frequency_number: In Nov he had 5 short seizures in a brief series. In Feb six daytime events were noted, in Mar 5 myoclonic jerks occurred, and in May three generalised tonic-clonic seizures
